message:
feat(search): federated multi-shard reader + publish manifest so SearchClient can query across staged lexical artifacts without a merge

Round-3 follow-up to the staged lexical rebuild finalizer
(commit 028db7ac). That commit taught the finalizer to assemble
multiple shard directories into a single index without re-tokenizing
docs. This commit teaches the reader side to open those shard
directories in parallel and federate queries across them via a
persisted manifest — useful when you don't want to (or can't)
assemble the full generation up front and would rather search the
shards in place.

src/search/tantivy.rs

- New on-disk manifest format, written into the index directory as
  `federated-search-manifest.json`:
  * FederatedSearchManifest { version: u32, kind: String,
    schema_hash: String, shards: Vec<FederatedSearchShardManifest> }
  * FederatedSearchShardManifest { relative_path, docs, segments,
    meta_fingerprint }. `meta_fingerprint` captures the segment
    set at publish time so drift can be detected.
- New public API:
  * `SearchableIndexSummary { docs, segments }` — the at-a-glance
    summary surfaced to callers.
  * `pub fn searchable_index_exists(&Path) -> bool` — cheap
    existence check: a singleton generation OR a federated manifest
    both count.
  * `pub fn searchable_index_modified_time(&Path) -> Option<SystemTime>`
    — returns the latest mtime across shard segment files (for
    cache invalidation vs the warm searcher).
  * `pub fn searchable_index_fingerprint(&Path) -> Result<Option<String>>`
    — combined fingerprint over schema_hash + per-shard
    meta_fingerprint so upstream code can detect any shard change
    without rehashing everything.
  * `pub fn searchable_index_summary(&Path) -> Result<Option<SearchableIndexSummary>>`
    — aggregates docs/segments across all shards into a single
    summary; `None` when no manifest or singleton is present.
  * `pub fn open_federated_search_readers(&Path) -> Result<Vec<(IndexReader, FsCassFields)>>`
    — opens a `FsReloadPolicy::Manual` reader per shard listed in
    the manifest, returning each with its own fields handle so
    query code can run parallel `search_with_weight`s.
  * `pub fn publish_federated_searchable_index_directories(index_path, inputs)`
    — writes the manifest file next to the shards: for each input
    directory, opens it, loads metas, computes the
    meta_fingerprint, records docs + segments, and appends a
    FederatedSearchShardManifest entry. Manifest is sorted by
    relative_path for deterministic hashing.
- `use` additions: ReloadPolicy as FsReloadPolicy,
  `cass_open_search_reader as fs_cass_open_search_reader`, serde
  Serialize/Deserialize, `std::time::SystemTime`,
  `std::path::PathBuf`.

src/search/query.rs

- `SearcherCacheEntry` gains a `reader_key: usize` field so
  per-thread searcher caches can be invalidated when the underlying
  reader changes (previously only `epoch` was tracked, which missed
  fingerprint-driven drops).
- New internal type `FederatedIndexReader { reader: IndexReader,
  fields: FsCassFields }` paired with a process-wide
  `FEDERATED_SEARCH_READERS: Lazy<RwLock<HashMap<String,
  Arc<Vec<FederatedIndexReader>>>>>`. Keyed by a cache-namespace
  string (`v{CACHE_KEY_VERSION}|schema:{FS_CASS_SCHEMA_HASH}|index:{path}`)
  so schema bumps, cache-key-version bumps, or index-path changes
  all trigger fresh reader construction.
- `SearchClient` impls:
  * `Drop for SearchClient`: on drop, remove this client's
    `cache_namespace` entry from `FEDERATED_SEARCH_READERS` so we
    don't leak per-client reader handles when a federated client is
    dropped.
  * Client construction now computes the `cache_namespace` up front
    and, when the primary `fs_cass_open_search_reader(index_path,
    ReloadPolicy::Manual)` returns None (no singleton generation),
    transparently falls back to opening the federated readers via
    the new `open_federated_search_readers` API and caching them in
    `FEDERATED_SEARCH_READERS` under the namespace.
- New `use` entries: `std::hash::{Hash, Hasher}` for the reader-key
  hashing.

Net effect: after a lexical rebuild publishes shards directly
(without running the final assembly step), SearchClient can still
return correct results; every shard contributes to the per-query
score sheet and the combined result set reflects the full archive,
not just the last shard.
